stack-anatomy.instruction: Map the layers of a full-stack AI app. For each layer (frontend, API, database, AI, deployment), name the specific tool you'd use and justify why it's the right fit for a solo developer.
stack-anatomy.hint: Frontend: Next.js — React-based, server-side rendering, great DX, Vercel integration. API: Next.js API routes or Supabase Edge Functions — serverless, zero ops. Database: Supabase PostgreSQL + pgvector — relational + vector in one. AI: Claude API — best reasoning, structured output. Deploy: Vercel — git push to production, preview deploys, free tier.
stack-anatomy.badExample: Frontend: React. API: Node. Database: MySQL. AI: ChatGPT. Deploy: AWS.
stack-anatomy.goodExample: Frontend: Next.js 14 — React framework with App Router, built-in API routes (no separate backend needed), server components for performance, and Vercel integration means zero-config deploys. For a solo dev, fewer moving parts = fewer things to break. API: Supabase Edge Functions (Deno) for webhook handlers and heavy processing, Next.js API routes for frontend-coupled endpoints. Why both: edge functions scale independently and have direct database access; API routes stay close to the UI code. Database: Supabase PostgreSQL with pgvector extension — one database handles users (relational), conversations (append-only), and embeddings (vector similarity search). No need to manage separate vector DB. AI: Claude API via Anthropic SDK — best at structured output and reasoning, which matters for production (parsing JSON responses reliably). Deploy: Vercel — git push deploys, preview URLs for every PR, serverless scaling. $0/month until you have real traffic.
stack-anatomy.successMessage: You just drew the architecture diagram in words. For solo developers, simplicity IS the competitive advantage.

stack-anatomy.instruction: Analyze this stack choice: A solo developer building an AI chatbot chose: React (no framework), Express.js, MongoDB, self-hosted Llama on a rented GPU, and manual deploys via SSH. Identify the 3 biggest risks.
stack-anatomy.hint: Risk 1: No framework (React alone) means building routing, SSR, and API layer from scratch — weeks of work Next.js gives for free. Risk 2: Self-hosted Llama on rented GPU = DevOps burden (monitoring, scaling, GPU costs) that a solo dev can't sustain. Risk 3: Manual SSH deploys = no rollback, no preview environments, and eventually a production-breaking deploy at 2am.
stack-anatomy.goodExample: 3 biggest risks for a solo dev: (1) SELF-HOSTED LLM = OPS NIGHTMARE — Renting a GPU ($2-8K/month), managing model updates, monitoring inference performance, handling cold starts, and debugging CUDA errors. That's a full-time DevOps job. An API call to Claude/GPT costs $0.01-0.05 per request and scales infinitely with zero ops. The GPU money buys 100K+ API calls/month. (2) NO FRAMEWORK TAX — Vanilla React means manually building: routing, server-side rendering, API layer, auth integration, and build pipeline. Next.js provides all of this. The solo dev will spend 2-3 weeks building infrastructure that exists for free. (3) SSH DEPLOYS = INEVITABLE DISASTER — No staging environment, no rollback button, no preview deploys. One bad deploy takes the site down, and the only fix is SSH-ing in at 2am. Vercel/Railway give automated deploys with instant rollback for $0-20/month. The pattern: this developer chose 'control' over 'convenience' — which makes sense at Netflix-scale but is a trap for solo builders.
stack-anatomy.successMessage: For solo developers, every self-hosted component is a liability, not an asset. Use managed services until you have the team to manage infrastructure.

supabase-101.instruction: Write a prompt to generate a Supabase SQL migration for a 'projects' table with proper constraints, foreign keys, and row-level security.
supabase-101.placeholder: Create a Supabase migration for a projects table...
supabase-101.hint: Create a Supabase SQL migration for a 'projects' table: id (uuid, primary key, default gen_random_uuid()), name (text, not null, max 255 chars), description (text, nullable), owner_id (uuid, foreign key to auth.users, not null), status (text, check constraint: 'active', 'archived', 'draft'), created_at (timestamptz, default now()), updated_at (timestamptz, default now()). Include RLS policy: users can only read/write their own projects.
supabase-101.badExample: Make a table for projects in Supabase.
supabase-101.goodExample: Create a production-ready Supabase SQL migration for a 'projects' table. Columns: id (uuid, primary key, gen_random_uuid()), name (text, not null, check length <= 255), description (text, nullable), owner_id (uuid, references auth.users(id) on delete cascade, not null), status (text, not null, default 'draft', check in ('draft', 'active', 'archived')), created_at (timestamptz, default now()), updated_at (timestamptz, default now()). Include: (1) Enable RLS. (2) SELECT policy: users see only their own projects. (3) INSERT policy: users can only create projects where owner_id = auth.uid(). (4) UPDATE policy: users can only update their own. (5) DELETE policy: users can only delete their own. (6) An index on owner_id for fast lookups. (7) A trigger to auto-update updated_at on row changes. Write as a complete migration file I can paste into Supabase SQL editor.
supabase-101.successMessage: Production-ready table design with security built in. RLS + proper constraints = defense in depth.

supabase-101.instruction: A developer's Supabase query returns zero rows even though they can see data in the Supabase dashboard. They're calling: `supabase.from('projects').select('*')` from their Next.js app. Diagnose the most likely cause.
supabase-101.hint: Row-Level Security (RLS) is enabled but no policy exists for the authenticated user, OR the user is not authenticated. The Supabase dashboard uses the service role key (bypasses RLS), but the client SDK uses the anon key (respects RLS). If there's no SELECT policy, authenticated users see nothing.
supabase-101.badExample: The database might be empty or the table name is wrong.
supabase-101.goodExample: Most likely cause: ROW-LEVEL SECURITY (RLS). Here's what's happening: (1) RLS is enabled on the 'projects' table (Supabase enables it by default). (2) There's no SELECT policy defined for authenticated users. (3) The Supabase dashboard uses the SERVICE ROLE key, which bypasses RLS entirely — so the developer sees all data there. (4) The Next.js app uses the ANON or authenticated client key, which respects RLS. With no policy granting SELECT access, the query returns zero rows — not an error, just no permitted results. Diagnostic steps: Run `select * from pg_policies where tablename = 'projects'` in the SQL editor. If no policies exist, that's the problem. Fix: Create a SELECT policy: `create policy "Users can view own projects" on projects for select using (auth.uid() = owner_id);`. Common variation: the user IS authenticated but their JWT doesn't contain the expected uid because the session expired or the auth client wasn't initialized properly.
supabase-101.successMessage: This is the #1 Supabase debugging gotcha. RLS working silently is a feature, not a bug — but it surprises every new developer.

vercel-deploy.instruction: Describe the complete deployment pipeline from git push to live site on Vercel. Include: what triggers the build, what happens during build, how env variables work, and how to rollback.
vercel-deploy.hint: Push to main -> Vercel webhook triggers -> build starts (install deps, run build command) -> static assets uploaded to CDN -> serverless functions deployed -> preview/production URL assigned. Env vars: set in Vercel dashboard, available at build time via process.env. Rollback: click 'Promote to Production' on any previous deployment in the dashboard.
vercel-deploy.badExample: Push code and Vercel deploys it.
vercel-deploy.goodExample: COMPLETE VERCEL DEPLOY PIPELINE: (1) TRIGGER: Developer pushes to GitHub. Vercel's GitHub integration detects the push via webhook. Push to 'main' = production deploy. Push to any other branch = preview deploy with a unique URL. (2) BUILD: Vercel clones the repo, installs dependencies (npm install), runs the build command (next build). During build: env variables from Vercel dashboard are injected as process.env.VARIABLE_NAME. Build output: static HTML/CSS/JS + serverless function bundles. (3) DEPLOY: Static assets uploaded to Vercel's global CDN (200+ edge locations). Serverless functions (API routes) deployed to AWS Lambda behind the scenes. Each deploy gets a unique URL: project-abc123.vercel.app. (4) ROUTING: If production deploy, the custom domain (yourdomain.com) points to this new deployment. Old deployment still exists but is no longer served. (5) ENV VARIABLES: Set in Vercel dashboard, Project Settings, Environment Variables. Can be scoped to Production, Preview, or Development. Secrets (API keys) never appear in build logs. (6) ROLLBACK: Every deployment is immutable and preserved. To rollback: open Vercel dashboard, Deployments, find the last working deploy, click 'Promote to Production.' Takes 5 seconds, zero downtime. (7) PREVIEW DEPLOYS: Every pull request gets its own URL. Perfect for testing before merging. Vercel adds a comment to the PR with the preview URL automatically.
vercel-deploy.successMessage: Understanding the deploy pipeline means you'll debug production issues in minutes instead of hours.

vercel-deploy.instruction:
This deployment process is dangerous. Rewrite it as a safe, modern pipeline:

'I edit files directly on the production server via FTP. When I make a mistake, I try to remember what I changed and undo it manually.'
vercel-deploy.hint: Replace FTP with git-based deployments, add preview environments, environment variables for secrets, and instant rollback via Vercel/Netlify.
vercel-deploy.badExample: Use FTP more carefully and keep backups.
vercel-deploy.goodExample: SAFE DEPLOYMENT PROCESS: (1) Code lives in Git (GitHub) — every change is tracked, every version recoverable. NEVER edit production directly. (2) Development: work on a feature branch, not main. Push to branch, Vercel creates a preview deploy with its own URL. Test there. (3) Review: open a pull request. Preview URL lets anyone test before merging. (4) Deploy: merge PR to main, Vercel auto-deploys to production. Zero manual steps, zero FTP, zero SSH. (5) Secrets: API keys stored in Vercel environment variables, not in code files. Never committed to git, never visible in build logs. (6) WHEN something breaks: click 'Rollback' in Vercel dashboard, instant revert to previous working deployment. No remembering what changed. No manual undoing. Takes 5 seconds. (7) Monitoring: Vercel shows build logs, runtime logs, and error tracking. You KNOW when something fails instead of waiting for a customer to email you. Migration path from FTP: (1) Put code in GitHub. (2) Connect repo to Vercel. (3) Set env variables. (4) Point domain to Vercel. Done in 30 minutes. Never FTP again.
vercel-deploy.successMessage: FTP to production is how websites died in the 2000s. Git-based deploys with preview environments and instant rollback is how professionals ship.

edge-functions.instruction: Write a prompt to create a Supabase Edge Function that receives a webhook, validates the payload, and inserts data into a table. Include error handling.
edge-functions.hint: Create a Supabase Edge Function in TypeScript/Deno that: (1) receives POST requests from a Stripe webhook, (2) validates the webhook signature using the Stripe secret, (3) parses the event type, (4) for 'checkout.session.completed' events, extracts customer email and amount, (5) inserts a record into the 'payments' table, (6) returns 200 on success, 400 on bad payload, 401 on invalid signature, 500 on database error.
edge-functions.badExample: Write a function that handles webhooks.
edge-functions.goodExample: Create a Supabase Edge Function (Deno/TypeScript) for Stripe webhook handling. Requirements: (1) Accept POST requests only — return 405 for other methods. (2) Validate Stripe webhook signature using STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET from environment. Return 401 if invalid. (3) Parse the JSON body. Return 400 if malformed. (4) Handle 'checkout.session.completed' events: extract customer_email, amount_total, currency, session_id. (5) Insert into 'payments' table: {stripe_session_id, email, amount, currency, created_at}. (6) Return 200 with {received: true} on success. (7) Return 500 with error details (sanitized — no stack traces) on database failure. (8) Log all events to console for Supabase log viewer. Include: proper CORS headers for the Supabase domain, try/catch around every I/O operation, and the Deno.serve() pattern.
edge-functions.successMessage: Edge functions are your serverless backend. Proper validation, error handling, and status codes make the difference between a toy and production.

edge-functions.instruction: Compare Supabase Edge Functions and Vercel Serverless Functions. When would you choose each? Consider: runtime, cold starts, database access, and deployment.
edge-functions.placeholder: Supabase Edge Functions: best when... Vercel Serverless: best when...
edge-functions.hint: Supabase Edge Functions: Deno runtime, deployed globally, direct Supabase DB access, best for webhooks and background processing. Vercel Serverless: Node.js runtime, tied to your Next.js app, best for API routes that serve the frontend. Choose Supabase for standalone functions, Vercel for frontend-coupled APIs.
edge-functions.goodExample: SUPABASE EDGE FUNCTIONS — Best when: (1) The function needs direct database access (Supabase client is built-in, no connection string management). (2) Processing webhooks from external services (Stripe, GitHub) — standalone, not tied to a frontend. (3) Background processing or cron-style tasks. (4) You want global edge deployment (Deno Deploy runs on 30+ regions). Gotchas: Deno runtime, not Node.js — some npm packages need compatibility wrappers. VERCEL SERVERLESS FUNCTIONS — Best when: (1) The function serves your Next.js frontend directly (API routes in app/api/). (2) You need Node.js runtime and npm ecosystem compatibility. (3) The function's code is tightly coupled to your frontend logic (shared types, utilities). (4) You want the simplest DX — it's just a file in your Next.js project. Gotchas: cold starts can be 200-500ms for infrequently called functions. MY RULE: If the function exists because of the frontend, use Vercel. If the function exists independently of the frontend (webhooks, background jobs, external integrations), use Supabase Edge. This keeps your architecture clean: Vercel serves users, Supabase processes events.
edge-functions.successMessage: Choosing the right serverless platform for each function keeps your architecture clean and your DX smooth.

database-patterns.instruction: Write a prompt to design Row-Level Security (RLS) policies for a 'documents' table with three access levels: users read/write their own, team members can read shared docs, and admins can read everything.
database-patterns.placeholder: Design RLS policies for a documents table...
database-patterns.hint: Table: documents (id, title, content, owner_id, team_id, is_shared, created_at). Policies: (1) Owner SELECT/UPDATE/DELETE: where owner_id = auth.uid(). (2) Team member SELECT: where team_id in (select team_id from team_members where user_id = auth.uid()) AND is_shared = true. (3) Admin SELECT: where exists (select 1 from profiles where id = auth.uid() and role = 'admin').
database-patterns.badExample: Make a security policy for the documents table.
database-patterns.goodExample: Design RLS policies for a 'documents' table in Supabase. Schema: documents (id uuid PK, title text, content text, owner_id uuid FK auth.users, team_id uuid FK teams, is_shared boolean default false, created_at timestamptz). Access levels: (1) OWNER: full CRUD on their own documents (owner_id = auth.uid()). (2) TEAM MEMBER: can SELECT documents that are is_shared=true AND belong to a team they're a member of (team_members junction table has user_id and team_id). (3) ADMIN: can SELECT all documents regardless of ownership or sharing. Admin role stored in profiles table (role column). Write all the SQL: enable RLS, create each policy with descriptive names, and explain the USING clause logic. Include the INSERT policy that ensures owner_id is always set to the authenticated user (prevents impersonation).
database-patterns.successMessage: RLS is security at the database layer — it can't be bypassed by frontend bugs. Secure by default, not by hope.

database-patterns.instruction: A developer has a 'messages' table with RLS enabled. The INSERT policy is: `create policy "Users can insert" on messages for insert with check (true)`. This seems to work but is actually a security hole. Identify the vulnerability and write the fix.
database-patterns.placeholder: The vulnerability is... The fix is...
database-patterns.hint: The policy allows ANY authenticated user to insert messages with ANY user_id — including impersonating other users. The 'with check (true)' means there are no constraints on what data gets inserted. Fix: `with check (auth.uid() = sender_id)` to ensure users can only insert messages as themselves.
database-patterns.badExample: The policy looks fine since it checks that the user is authenticated.
database-patterns.goodExample: VULNERABILITY: The policy `with check (true)` only verifies the user is authenticated — it doesn't restrict WHAT they can insert. An attacker can insert messages with any sender_id, effectively impersonating other users. They could also set any conversation_id, injecting messages into conversations they're not part of. Proof: A malicious user could call `supabase.from('messages').insert({sender_id: 'some-other-user-id', conversation_id: 'private-convo', content: 'fake message'})` and it would succeed. FIX: `create policy "Users insert own messages" on messages for insert with check (auth.uid() = sender_id AND conversation_id in (select conversation_id from conversation_members where user_id = auth.uid()));` This ensures: (1) sender_id MUST match the authenticated user (no impersonation). (2) The user must be a member of the conversation they're posting to (no injection into private conversations). General rule: INSERT policies should always verify ownership of the identity column AND access to related resources.
database-patterns.successMessage: RLS INSERT policies with `check (true)` are one of the most common security holes in Supabase apps. Always constrain the identity column.

auth-and-tokens.instruction: Explain the complete JWT authentication flow in a Supabase app: from login to authenticated API call. Cover what tokens exist, where they're stored, and how they're refreshed.
auth-and-tokens.hint: 1. User enters email/password or clicks OAuth. 2. Supabase Auth verifies credentials and returns two tokens: access token (JWT, short-lived ~1hr) and refresh token (long-lived). 3. Supabase client stores both in localStorage or cookies. 4. Every API call sends the access token in the Authorization header. 5. Supabase verifies the JWT and extracts the user_id for RLS. 6. When the access token expires, the client silently uses the refresh token to get a new access token.
auth-and-tokens.badExample: The user logs in and gets a token that authenticates them.
auth-and-tokens.goodExample: SUPABASE JWT AUTH FLOW: (1) LOGIN: User submits credentials (email/password, magic link, or OAuth like Google). Supabase Auth verifies them against the auth.users table. (2) TOKEN ISSUANCE: On success, Supabase returns TWO tokens: Access token (JWT) — contains user_id, email, role. Short-lived: expires in 1 hour. This is what authenticates every request. Refresh token — opaque string, long-lived (days/weeks). Used only to get new access tokens. (3) STORAGE: The Supabase JS client stores both tokens automatically. In browsers: localStorage by default (can be configured to use cookies for SSR). The developer doesn't manually manage tokens. (4) AUTHENTICATED REQUESTS: Every `supabase.from('table').select()` call automatically includes the access token in the `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header. (5) SERVER-SIDE VERIFICATION: Supabase's PostgREST layer verifies the JWT signature (using the project's JWT secret), checks expiration, and extracts `auth.uid()` — which is used in RLS policies. No database lookup needed — the JWT is self-contained. (6) TOKEN REFRESH: When the access token expires, the Supabase client automatically sends the refresh token to get a new access token. This happens silently — the user stays logged in. (7) LOGOUT: Both tokens are deleted client-side. The refresh token is revoked server-side.
auth-and-tokens.successMessage: Auth is the most important part of your stack to understand deeply. Every API call and RLS policy depends on this flow.

auth-and-tokens.instruction: A developer's Supabase app works perfectly in development but in production, authenticated users randomly get logged out. The session seems to disappear. Diagnose the likely causes.
auth-and-tokens.hint: Common causes: (1) localStorage is being cleared by user or browser privacy settings. (2) The site URL in Supabase Auth settings doesn't match the production domain, causing redirect loops. (3) CORS issues prevent the token refresh call. (4) Server-side rendering (SSR) doesn't have access to client-side localStorage tokens.
auth-and-tokens.badExample: The session timeout is probably too short.
auth-and-tokens.goodExample: 4 likely causes for random logouts in production: (1) SSR/CLIENT MISMATCH — If using Next.js with server-side rendering, the server doesn't have access to localStorage tokens. The page renders as 'logged out' on the server, then hydrates as 'logged in' on the client, causing flicker or route protection failures. Fix: use Supabase SSR helpers with cookie-based sessions instead of localStorage. (2) SITE URL MISMATCH — Supabase Auth settings have a 'Site URL' and 'Redirect URLs.' If these are set to localhost or a different domain, OAuth redirects and token refresh calls fail silently in production. Fix: update to your production domain in Supabase dashboard, Auth, URL Configuration. (3) TOKEN REFRESH FAILURE — If the refresh token call fails (network error, CORS issue, or Supabase being momentarily unreachable), the access token expires and the user is logged out with no recovery. Fix: implement retry logic and handle the TOKEN_REFRESHED and SIGNED_OUT auth events. (4) BROWSER STORAGE CLEARED — Safari's Intelligent Tracking Prevention and some ad blockers clear localStorage for 'third-party' contexts. If your Supabase URL is on a different domain than your app, tokens may be cleared. Fix: use cookie-based storage or a same-domain proxy.
auth-and-tokens.successMessage: Production auth issues are almost always about token storage and refresh — things that 'just work' in development because you're on localhost.

make-com-101.instruction: Design a Make.com scenario that connects your AI app to an external service. Include the trigger, processing modules, data mapping, and error handling.
make-com-101.hint: Scenario: When a new row appears in a Google Sheet (trigger) -> parse the row data (data transform) -> send the text column to Claude API for summarization (HTTP module) -> insert the summary into Supabase (HTTP module) -> send a Slack notification with the result (Slack module). Error handling: if Claude fails, retry once, then log to an error sheet.
make-com-101.badExample: Connect Google Sheets to my database using Make.
make-com-101.goodExample: Design a Make.com scenario for automated content processing: TRIGGER: 'Watch New Rows' in Google Sheet — fires when a new row is added to the 'Content Ideas' sheet. Data available: title, description, target_audience. MODULE 1 (Data Transform): Format the data into a Claude API prompt: 'You are a content strategist. Given this idea: [title] — [description], for audience: [target_audience], generate: a 3-bullet outline, 5 SEO keywords, and estimated word count. Return as JSON.' MODULE 2 (HTTP Request): POST to Claude API with the formatted prompt. Parse the JSON response. MODULE 3 (HTTP Request): POST to Supabase REST API — insert into 'content_plans' table: {title, outline, keywords, word_count, status: 'planned'}. MODULE 4 (Slack): Send message to #content channel: 'New content plan: [title] — [word_count] words. Outline: [3 bullets].' ERROR HANDLING: If Module 2 fails (Claude API error), wait 30 seconds and retry once. If retry fails, add a row to 'Errors' sheet with the failed data and error message. If Module 3 fails (Supabase error), send alert to #engineering Slack channel.
make-com-101.successMessage: Make.com is the glue layer between services. You just designed a real production integration with proper error handling.

make-com-101.instruction: Analyze when to use Make.com/Zapier versus building a custom integration with code. Give 2 scenarios where no-code wins and 2 where code is better.
make-com-101.placeholder: No-code wins when... Code wins when...
make-com-101.hint: No-code wins: (1) simple triggers like 'new email -> Slack notification' — building this in code would take hours for something Make does in 5 minutes. (2) Non-developer needs to modify the workflow — visual editor is accessible. Code wins: (1) complex logic with loops, conditionals, and error handling that Make's modules can't express. (2) High-volume processing where per-execution pricing makes Make expensive.
make-com-101.badExample: Always use code because it's more flexible.
make-com-101.goodExample: NO-CODE WINS: (1) Straightforward service-to-service glue — 'When a form is submitted, create a CRM contact, send a welcome email, and notify Slack.' Building this in code means writing 4 API integrations, handling auth for each, error handling, retry logic, and hosting. Make does it in 15 minutes with drag-and-drop. (2) When non-developers need to modify the workflow — a marketing manager can adjust the Slack notification without filing an engineering ticket. CODE WINS: (1) Complex processing with branching logic — 'Analyze 500 customer records, classify each into 3 tiers using AI, route each tier differently, aggregate results, and generate a report.' Make's visual interface becomes unmanageable with this many branches. Code handles it in 50 lines. (2) High-volume or latency-sensitive processing — Make charges per operation ($9/month for 10K ops). If you process 100K events/day, that's $90+/month on Make vs. $0.50 on a Supabase Edge Function. Also, Make adds 100-500ms per module, which compounds across a 5-module scenario. THE RULE: If you can build it in Make in under 30 minutes and volume is under 10K/month, use Make. Otherwise, write code.
make-com-101.successMessage: The best builders use both: no-code for simple integrations and code for complex/high-volume work. Choosing wisely saves time and money.

webhooks-deep-dive.instruction: A webhook is failing silently — no errors, but data isn't appearing in your database. Design a systematic debugging checklist: what to check first, second, third, with specific tools.
webhooks-deep-dive.hint: 1. Is the webhook being sent? Check the sender's webhook delivery logs. 2. Is it reaching your endpoint? Check server/function logs. 3. Is the URL correct and accessible? Test with curl. 4. Is the payload format correct? Log and inspect the raw body. 5. Is the database insert succeeding? Check for RLS policy blocks or schema mismatches.
webhooks-deep-dive.badExample: Check if the webhook URL is right.
webhooks-deep-dive.goodExample: WEBHOOK DEBUGGING CHECKLIST (systematic, never skip steps): CHECK 1 — IS IT BEING SENT? Tool: Sender's webhook dashboard (Stripe Dashboard, Webhooks, Recent events). Look for: delivery attempts, HTTP status codes returned, timestamps. If no attempts: the trigger condition isn't met or the webhook isn't configured. CHECK 2 — IS IT REACHING YOUR SERVER? Tool: Supabase Function logs (`supabase functions logs <name>`) or Vercel Deployments, Function Logs. Add a console.log at the very first line of the function. If nothing logs: the URL is wrong, there's a DNS issue, or a firewall/CORS block. CHECK 3 — IS THE URL CORRECT? Tool: curl from your terminal: `curl -X POST https://your-endpoint.com/webhook -d '{"test":true}' -H 'Content-Type: application/json'`. Should return your expected response code. If error: URL typo, function not deployed, SSL issue. CHECK 4 — IS THE PAYLOAD WHAT YOU EXPECT? Tool: console.log(JSON.stringify(request.body)) in your function. Compare the actual payload structure to what your code expects. Common gotcha: nested objects where you expect flat, or camelCase vs snake_case mismatches. CHECK 5 — IS THE DATABASE INSERT WORKING? Tool: Supabase SQL editor — check the table directly. If the function logs show success but the table is empty: RLS is blocking the insert (use the service role key for server-side inserts, not the anon key). CHECK 6 — IS THERE A TIMING ISSUE? Tool: add timestamps to your logs. Webhooks can arrive before your database has the referenced record. Fix: upsert or retry with backoff.
webhooks-deep-dive.successMessage: Systematic webhook debugging saves hours. The key: isolate which LINK in the chain is broken before trying fixes.

webhooks-deep-dive.hint: Missing: signature verification, payload validation, error handling, idempotency, proper status codes, logging, and rate limiting.
webhooks-deep-dive.badExample: Add a try-catch around the insert.
webhooks-deep-dive.goodExample: Production-ready webhook handler must add: (1) SIGNATURE VERIFICATION — verify the webhook comes from the expected sender. Stripe, GitHub, etc. all send a signature header. `const isValid = verifySignature(req.headers['stripe-signature'], req.body, WEBHOOK_SECRET);` Return 401 if invalid. Without this, anyone can POST fake data to your endpoint. (2) PAYLOAD VALIDATION — parse and validate the body structure before inserting. Check required fields exist, types are correct, and values are within expected ranges. Return 400 for malformed payloads. (3) IDEMPOTENCY — webhooks are often sent multiple times (retries). Use a unique event ID: `if (await eventAlreadyProcessed(req.body.id)) return res.status(200)`. Without this, you'll create duplicate records. (4) ERROR HANDLING — wrap everything in try/catch. Never return 500 to a webhook sender unless you want infinite retries. Return 200 even if processing fails, but log the error and add to a retry queue. (5) PROPER STATUS CODES — 200 for success, 401 for bad signature, 400 for bad payload, 202 for 'accepted but processing async.' (6) LOGGING — log every webhook: event type, ID, timestamp, processing result. This is your audit trail. (7) RESPOND FAST — webhook senders typically timeout after 5-30 seconds. If your processing takes longer, return 202 immediately and process async (queue).
webhooks-deep-dive.successMessage: The difference between a toy webhook handler and a production one: signature verification, idempotency, and proper error handling.

wire-it-all.instruction: Trace the complete data flow for an AI feature: user clicks 'Generate' to when the AI response appears. Cover frontend, API, AI service, database, and response — with error handling at each step.
wire-it-all.hint: User clicks -> frontend sends POST to API route -> API validates auth + input -> API calls Claude with system prompt + user input -> Claude returns response -> API saves to database -> API returns response to frontend -> frontend renders the result. Errors: frontend shows loading state, API returns structured error codes, database failures don't lose the AI response.
wire-it-all.badExample: User clicks button, AI generates text, user sees it.
wire-it-all.goodExample: COMPLETE DATA FLOW — 'Generate Summary' Feature: (1) USER ACTION: User pastes text and clicks 'Generate Summary.' Frontend immediately shows a loading skeleton (not a spinner). (2) FRONTEND to API: React component calls `fetch('/api/summarize', {method: 'POST', body: {text, userId}})`. Error handling: if fetch fails (network error), show 'Connection error, retrying...' and retry once. (3) API VALIDATION: API route checks: is the user authenticated? (Check JWT). Is the input valid? (Text length between 50-50,000 chars). Is the user within their usage quota? Return 401/400/429 with specific error codes. (4) API to AI: Construct the prompt: system prompt + user text. Call Claude API with timeout of 30 seconds. Error handling: if Claude returns 429 (rate limit), wait and retry. If 500, return a cached/fallback response or a user-friendly error. (5) AI to API: Parse Claude's response. Validate it matches expected format. (6) API to DATABASE: Save to 'summaries' table: {user_id, input_hash, output, model_used, tokens_used, created_at}. Error handling: if DB insert fails, still return the AI response to the user (don't lose work), but log the DB error for investigation. (7) API to FRONTEND: Return JSON: {summary, wordCount, tokensUsed}. HTTP 200. (8) FRONTEND RENDER: Replace loading skeleton with the summary. Show a 'Copy' button and a 'Save' confirmation. If the API returned an error, show a user-friendly message with a 'Try Again' button. TOTAL LATENCY: ~2-5 seconds (mostly Claude API time). Use streaming to show text appearing word-by-word for perceived speed.
wire-it-all.successMessage: End-to-end thinking separates full-stack builders from tutorial followers. The error handling at each layer is what makes it production-ready.

wire-it-all.instruction: Write a prompt to design the error handling strategy for a full-stack AI app. Cover every layer: frontend, API, AI service, and database.
wire-it-all.placeholder: Design error handling for my AI app across all layers...
wire-it-all.hint: My app: Next.js frontend + Supabase API + Claude AI + PostgreSQL database. Design error handling for: (1) Frontend: network failures, loading states, user-friendly error messages. (2) API: input validation, auth errors, rate limiting. (3) AI: timeout, rate limit, hallucination detection, cost overrun. (4) Database: connection failures, RLS blocks, constraint violations. For each: the error, the user-facing message, the developer-facing log, and the recovery strategy.
wire-it-all.badExample: How should I handle errors in my app?
wire-it-all.goodExample: Design a comprehensive error handling strategy for my full-stack AI app (Next.js + Supabase + Claude API + PostgreSQL). For each layer, define: the error types, user-facing message, developer-facing log, and recovery strategy. FRONTEND ERRORS: network failures, timeout, render errors. API ERRORS: 400 (bad input), 401 (auth), 403 (forbidden), 429 (rate limit), 500 (server). AI SERVICE ERRORS: Claude timeout (>30s), rate limiting (429), malformed response (JSON parse failure), content filter triggered, unexpected token count (cost overrun). DATABASE ERRORS: connection refused, RLS policy block (silent empty result), unique constraint violation, foreign key violation. For each error: (1) What does the user see? (no technical jargon). (2) What gets logged? (full error context for debugging). (3) What's the automatic recovery? (retry, fallback, graceful degradation). Present as a table: Layer | Error | User Message | Log | Recovery.
wire-it-all.successMessage: Error handling across all layers is what separates apps that survive production from apps that crash at the first unexpected input.

launch-checklist.instruction: Create a pre-launch checklist for your AI app covering: security, performance, monitoring, error handling, and UX. At least 15 items, each with a specific action to verify it.
launch-checklist.hint: Security: env vars not in code, RLS enabled, API keys rotated, CORS restricted. Performance: images optimized, API responses cached, CDN configured. Monitoring: error tracking setup, uptime alerts, cost alerts for AI API. Error handling: all API routes return structured errors, frontend shows user-friendly messages. UX: loading states, mobile responsive, accessible.
launch-checklist.badExample: Make sure everything works and deploy.
launch-checklist.goodExample: PRE-LAUNCH CHECKLIST: SECURITY (5 items): [ ] All API keys in Vercel environment variables — none hardcoded, none in .env committed to git. Verify: `grep -r 'sk-' src/` returns nothing. [ ] RLS enabled on ALL tables — verify with: `select tablename from pg_tables where schemaname='public'` cross-referenced with `select tablename from pg_policies`. Every table must have policies. [ ] CORS restricted to production domain only — no wildcard `*` in production. [ ] Rate limiting on all AI-calling endpoints — prevent cost bombing. Max 10 requests/minute per user. [ ] Input validation on every API route — max length, type checking, sanitization. PERFORMANCE (4 items): [ ] Lighthouse score >90 on mobile — run `npx lighthouse https://yourdomain.com`. [ ] AI responses cached for identical queries — semantic cache or exact-match cache. [ ] Images optimized — next/image with WebP, lazy loading below fold. [ ] Database indexes on frequently queried columns — at minimum: user_id, created_at. MONITORING (3 items): [ ] Error tracking live (Sentry or Vercel Analytics) — test by throwing a deliberate error. [ ] AI API cost alerts — set a daily budget alert at 2x expected spend. [ ] Uptime monitoring — external service pinging /api/health every 5 minutes. ERROR HANDLING (2 items): [ ] Every API route has try/catch with structured error responses — no raw stack traces sent to client. [ ] Frontend shows user-friendly error messages with recovery actions — 'Try again' button, not 'Error 500.' UX (3 items): [ ] Loading states on every AI interaction — streaming or skeleton UI, never a bare spinner for >2 seconds. [ ] Mobile responsive — test on actual phone, not just browser devtools. [ ] Favicon, meta tags, and Open Graph image set — your app looks professional when shared on social media.
launch-checklist.successMessage: This checklist is your insurance policy against launch-day disasters. Every unchecked item is a risk; every checked item is confidence.

launch-checklist.instruction: Write a prompt asking Claude to review your app's architecture and find the single most likely point of failure that would cause you to lose users or revenue.
launch-checklist.placeholder: Review my stack and identify the biggest risk...
launch-checklist.hint: My stack: Next.js on Vercel free tier, Supabase Pro ($25/month), Claude API (~$50/month for 5,000 requests), Stripe for payments. 100 users, $500 MRR. No error tracking, no backup strategy, no fallback if Claude API goes down. What is the single biggest risk?
launch-checklist.goodExample: Review my app stack and identify the single most dangerous point of failure — the one thing most likely to cause user-facing downtime or revenue loss. My stack: Next.js on Vercel Pro ($20/month), Supabase Pro ($25/month with daily backups), Claude API via Anthropic (~$50/month, 5,000 requests), Stripe for subscriptions ($500 MRR, 100 paying users). Current gaps I know about: (1) No error tracking — I check logs manually. (2) No AI fallback — if Claude API is down, the core feature is dead. (3) No rate limiting — a bot could drain my AI budget. (4) Database migrations are manual SQL — no version control. Analyze: What's the most likely failure scenario? What's the impact? And what's the cheapest fix I can implement this weekend? Be brutally honest.
launch-checklist.successMessage: Every stack has a weakest link. Finding it before your users do is the highest-leverage engineering work you can do before launch.
